title: SWIFT CSCF 6.1 — malware protection across the SWIFT secure zone
exclusions: Does NOT substitute for the full regulatory control set; this UC provides one of several evidence streams and must be combined with policy, procedure, and supplemental UCs to demonstrate the complete clause.
evidence: Scheduled search output persisted to the compliance summary index with regulation=SWIFT CSP, clause=6.1, available as a saved search and exportable as a CSV evidence pack for the responsible officer.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for 6.1.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that SWIFT CSP 6.1 (Malware protection) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.50.13: SWIFT CSCF 6.1 — malware protection across the SWIFT secure zone.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.50.13' running on Endpoint detection and response (EDR), antivirus logs from the SWIFT secure zone, CSP attestation evidence., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.
compliance.smeCaveat: Phase-C draft: SME review required before uplifting assurance from 'contributing' to 'partial' or 'full'. Confirm that the SPL captures the specific regulator expectation and that the telemetry exists in the deployment.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'SWIFT CSCF 6.1 — malware protection across the SWIFT secure ' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper SWIFT CSP control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the SWIFT CSP control objective.

dataSources: Endpoint detection and response (EDR), antivirus logs from the SWIFT secure zone, CSP attestation evidence.
spl:
index=swift_zone earliest=-24h
| search (sourcetype="av" OR sourcetype="edr")
| stats count AS events count(eval(signature_coverage_hours <= 24)) AS current_coverage count(eval(action="block")) AS blocks BY host, edr_vendor
| eval up_to_date=if(current_coverage=events, "yes", "no")
| where up_to_date="no" OR blocks > 0
description: Confirms every SWIFT-secure-zone host is running malware protection with current signatures, evidencing CSCF 6.1.
value: CSCF 6.1 is a mandatory control; continuous coverage checks produce annual KYC-SA attestation evidence on demand.
implementation: (1) Identify the SWIFT secure zone with a 'zone=swift' asset field; (2) route EDR logs through a dedicated source; (3) schedule daily. Indexes required: `index=swift_zone`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=av`, `sourcetype=edr`.
visualization: Table of flagged records for investigation; single-value KPI of coverage / compliance percentage; time chart of trend over the last 90 days; drill-down per responsible owner. Key fields: `blocks`, `current_coverage`, `edr_vendor`, `events`, `host`, `up_to_date`.

references.title: SWIFT Customer Security Control Framework (CSCF) v2025

knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `swift_zone` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `6.1`. The control objective is: "Evidence that SWIFT CSP 6.1 (Malware protection) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.50.13: SWIFT CSCF 6.1 — malware protection across the SWIFT secure zone." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `swift_zone` under `av`, `edr`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Three operational considerations, authored alongside this UC, apply:


• **Hint 1:** Identify the SWIFT secure zone with a 'zone=swift' asset field. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** route EDR logs through a dedicated source. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 3:** schedule daily. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

The detection fires when `signature_coverage_hours <= 24`. For "SWIFT CSCF 6.1 — malware protection across the SWIFT secure zone," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the SWIFT CSP control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time. This threshold is the operational heart of the control — set it too low and the signal drowns in noise; set it too high and real compliance deviations go undetected. The value `24` should never be treated as universal; it must be calibrated against your specific operational baseline.

Calibration procedure: before enabling this as a production alert, run the base search over the last 30 days with the threshold removed and compute distribution statistics:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats count by signature_coverage_hours
| stats avg(signature_coverage_hours) as mean, perc50(signature_coverage_hours) as p50, perc90(signature_coverage_hours) as p90, perc99(signature_coverage_hours) as p99, max(signature_coverage_hours) as hi
```

Set the production threshold between p90 and p99 depending on your organisation's alert fatigue tolerance. Document the chosen value in the control specification alongside the date of calibration and the sample size — auditors will ask how you arrived at `24` and the defensible answer is 'data, plus a documented review by the control owner,' not 'the template said so.'

The `count()` aggregation over the last 24 hours is a deliberate choice. Compliance reports on sustained behaviour, not instantaneous spikes — a single noisy event crossing the threshold for one second should not page the on-call. If your organisation requires point-in-time sensitivity (e.g. real-time fraud detection), shorten the time window and aggregation span; for accountability reporting, widen them. Tune for the regulation's actual expectation, not engineering preference.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.50.13: SWIFT CSCF 6.1 — malware protection across the SWIFT secure ]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.50.13,reg=SWIFT_CSP
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=swift_zone sourcetype=av earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `blocks`, `current_coverage`, `edr_vendor`, `events`, `host`, `up_to_date`. Run:

```spl
index=swift_zone sourcetype=av earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table blocks, current_coverage, edr_vendor, events, host, up_to_date
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `signature_coverage_hours`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(signature_coverage_hours) as p50 perc90(signature_coverage_hours) as p90 perc99(signature_coverage_hours) as p99 max(signature_coverage_hours) as hi
```

The current threshold `24` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.50.13: SWIFT CSP evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Flagged Records For Investigation; Single-Value Kpi Of Coverage / Compliance Percentage; Time Chart Of Trend Over The Last 90 Days; Drill-Down Per Responsible Owner (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Too many or too few alerts:** The threshold is almost certainly miscalibrated for your estate — re-run the percentile calibration (Step 3d) on a representative 30-day window and pick a value between p90 and p99.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**SWIFT CSP-specific failure modes:**

• CSCF (Customer Security Controls Framework) Mandatory vs Advisory: CSCF distinguishes mandatory (must implement) from advisory. Detections must tag which category each finding belongs to — advisory findings don't prevent attestation.

• Architecture Type A vs B: SWIFT defines two architecture types. Detections must know which architecture applies; the same control has different implementation expectations per architecture.

• Swift CSP attestation cadence: annual attestation on July 31 anchoring point. Detections producing attestation evidence must align to this annual cycle; quarterly evidence is over-frequent, daily is too granular.

• Controls shifted mandatory (framework drift): SWIFT moves controls from advisory to mandatory each year. Reconcile UC-to-framework mapping annually as of the latest CSCF version.
grandmaExplanation: We help you show how a second-line framework line maps to what you actually monitor, so your gap story stays clear.
